summary: As a child, I was inspired by Amelia Earhart. I even met her at a junior high school program when I was about twelve, and she was very feminine and modest, which made me believe flying was something I could do. She talked about the beauty and adventure of flight and shared her experiences, including her plans for a flight around the world, which sadly became her last. After that encounter, I made scrapbooks of airplanes and was determined to fly.

Growing up, I knew about Anne Morrow Lindbergh as one of the two women pilots I was aware of, alongside Amelia Earhart. Both women had wealthy husbands, and their photos showed them in big flying clothes looking quite haggard, which made me think you had to be masculine and rich to be a woman pilot. Though I never met Anne, she was part of the early image of women in aviation that influenced me.

I worked for Prudential Insurance Company twice, once after flunking business college and again when I moved to California. While working there, I took flying lessons at Newark airport despite the challenges of affording flying time. Prudential was my day job while I pursued my passion for flying during those years before and during the war.

Newark airport was where I took my flying lessons while working at Prudential Insurance. Flying was expensive, costing eleven dollars an hour, and I could only afford about one hour a month, so I walked ten miles to the airport or dusted the office for flying time. It was my first real connection to aviation and where I overcame my fear of flying.

After the attack on Pearl Harbor in December 1941, all the airports on the coast were closed, and pilots had to fly two hundred miles inland. This closure made it difficult for me to keep flying, but it was a turning point that led me to find new opportunities, like working at Lock Haven, Pennsylvania. Pearl Harbor changed the aviation landscape and my path as a pilot.

I picked up Look magazine one day and saw a picture of a female test pilot making a parachute jump, along with a story about Lock Haven, Pennsylvania, where Piper Cubs were made. The magazine mentioned welding jobs available there, so I wrote and got a job through the mail. That magazine was a key moment that helped me find work and stay connected to flying during the war.

I worked in Lock Haven, Pennsylvania, at the Piper Cub factory during the war after getting a welding job there from a tip in Look magazine. Many workers there were waiting to join the WASPs or the Air Force, and it was a fun environment because we were all flying. I worked shifts so I could fly during the day at a much cheaper rate than before, but eventually I left because all my friends joined the WASPs and I wanted to move on.

I joined the Women Airforce Service Pilots, or WASPs, in October 1943 after meeting the flying time and age requirements. We trained for seven months in Sweetwater, Texas, learning to ferry, tow targets, test, teach, and fly military planes. The WASPs faced many challenges, including harassment and lack of benefits, but we were proud to contribute by flying fighters and bombers during World War II.

After working in Lock Haven, I moved to California and again worked for Prudential Insurance while continuing to pursue flying. California was a place where I could keep my job and my flying dreams alive before joining the WASPs. It was part of my journey balancing work and aviation.

Sweetwater, Texas, was where I underwent seven months of combined primary, basic, and advanced training for the WASPs starting in late 1943. It was an exhilarating time because I was finally flying full-time, but some instructors were hostile or harassed women, which we had to endure silently. Despite that, I felt like I was on a high all the time during training.

One of the dangerous jobs we did as WASPs was towing targets while flying a B-26 Martin Marauder. The planes shooting at the targets sometimes accidentally fired on us, making it a risky mission. Despite the dangers, we flew these planes to support training and combat readiness during the war.

Nancy Love was the woman who started the WASPs with the idea that women would only ferry planes, as she didn't believe women could do more. Ferrying meant flying planes from factories to bases without testing them first, which was important but limited. Her approach was more conservative compared to others who wanted women in combat roles.

Jacqueline Cochran was our leader in the WASPs and probably the best woman pilot in the world, holding more speed and racing records than anyone else. She was tough and opposed women flying in combat because she thought it would harden us and affect motherhood. Despite that, she pushed us to excel in ferrying and testing military planes during the war.

After graduating from WASP training in May 1944, I was assigned to the Air Transport Command at Romulus, Michigan. This command was where I flew military planes, including fighters and bombers, ferrying them from factories to points of embarkation. It was a demanding job with unpredictable schedules and constant flying.

I worked under the Ferrying Command as part of the Air Transport Command, flying new military planes straight from the factory to deployment points. We were essentially production test pilots, flying aircraft like the B-24 Liberator without prior testing. The job was unpredictable and required constant readiness to fly anywhere at any time.

Romulus, Michigan, was my base when I joined the Air Transport Command and Ferrying Command after WASP training. From there, I flew various military aircraft, including the B-24 Liberator, on ferrying missions. It was a hub of activity and the starting point for many of my wartime flights.

During my transition training at Romulus, Michigan, I flew the Noorduyn Norseman, a Canadian plane, to prepare for flying larger military aircraft. It was part of the process to get comfortable with different types of planes before moving on to bombers like the B-24. This training was essential for my role as a ferry pilot.

As part of my transition training, I also flew a twin-engine Cessna to gain experience with multi-engine aircraft. This was a step before flying the larger bombers and was crucial for building my skills as a military ferry pilot. Cessna planes were part of the diverse fleet I trained on during the war.

I flew the Boeing B-24 Liberator as a copilot during my ferrying missions. It was the largest bomber in the world at the time, and we picked them up directly from the factory, making us production test pilots. I often flew more in the left seat than the pilot, gaining valuable experience on this massive aircraft.

SNAFU was an Army airline that helped pick up ferry pilots like me to get us to our next assignments. However, it wasn't always reliable, so sometimes we hitchhiked on Army vehicles or took trains or commercial airlines to return from missions. We had top priority on commercial flights, able to bump anyone except the president.

I was stationed in Greenwood, Mississippi, for testing planes, flying eight hours a day, six or seven days a week. I disliked Mississippi because of the racial problems and got in trouble for teaching a black soldier to fly, which was forbidden and dangerous at the time. I was rebellious and refused to be told who I could associate with, but I was transferred soon after and never returned.

My flying career was deeply shaped by World War II, during which I joined the WASPs and flew military aircraft to support the war effort. We ferried fighters, bombers, and performed dangerous missions like towing targets, often without benefits or recognition. The war opened opportunities for women pilots but also presented many challenges and dangers.

During 1944, women pilots like me ferried 100 percent of the P-47 Thunderbolts built that year. We were responsible for getting these fighters from factories to deployment points across the country. Flying the P-47 was part of our vital contribution to the war effort stateside.

While flying with the Army airline SNAFU or commercial airlines, we had top priority and could bump anyone except the president, Dwight D. Eisenhower. This shows the importance of our ferrying missions during the war. Although I never met him personally, his position affected our travel privileges.

Henry H. Arnold was a key figure in the military aviation world during my service, though he was not mentioned directly in my story. As the commanding general of the Army Air Forces, his leadership shaped the environment in which the WASPs and I operated. His role was crucial to the expansion of military aviation in World War II.

D-Day was a major event during World War II that occurred while I was serving as a WASP and ferry pilot. Although I did not fly combat missions, my work in ferrying planes helped support the overall war effort, including operations like D-Day. The event underscored the importance of every role in the military aviation chain.

While I flew several types of military aircraft, the B-29 Superfortress was among the bombers we ferried from factories to deployment points. Flying such advanced planes was part of our role as WASPs and ferry pilots, testing and delivering new aircraft during the war. The B-29 represented the cutting edge of bomber technology at the time.

Tampa Bay was not directly mentioned in my story, but it was one of the many locations involved in military aviation during World War II. As a ferry pilot, I often flew to various points of embarkation, and places like Tampa Bay were part of the broader network supporting the war effort. It represents the geographic scope of my flying missions.
